description: A person or group can hold a 'sherelo' under a moral principle, law, or another stated source. This normative claim places a duty or limit on conduct or institutions and can still be spoken while its recognition is disputed. 'shereni' (entitlement) begins with a socially recognised scope; recognition can secure a right in practice without being the source of every right.
articulatory_notes: Raise the tongue blade behind the alveolar ridge for unvoiced 'sh' and release into middle-height front 'e'. Make a brief alveolar tap or short trill before a second, stressed middle-height front 'e'. Touch the tongue tip at the alveolar ridge for lateral 'l' and finish on middle-height rounded 'o'.
sound_symbolism: The first two syllables place 'sherelo' beside 'sherewa' (claim) and 'shereni' (entitlement) by sound. Its final lateral opens into rounded 'o' and gives the normative claim a spacious ending of its own.
usage_notes: The noun is countable. When a right's source or bearer matters, ordinary clauses supply them; recognition and fulfilment remain separate events. 'shereni' is an entitlement recognised within a social order, while bodily direction uses 'kuri'.

examples.phi: theula miona lo kolo sherelo phelu.

pillars.solarpunk-values: A right can place durable limits around access to shared necessities or participation when goodwill fails.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: The word keeps a denied claim audible when an institution rejects it; recognition and enforcement remain facts a speaker can state separately.

semantic_domains.communication: a claim whose source and fulfillment can be examined
semantic_domains.community: a normative claim held among people, groups, or institutions
semantic_domains.ethics: a claim that can place a duty or limit on conduct
